item.title: Canada moves to enshrine early access to work permits for asylum seekers
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Ottawa, asylum claimants, work permits, Immigration, Regulations

Summary: On June 19, 2026, Ottawa announced plans to permanently allow asylum claimants faster access to work permits through proposed changes to the Immigration and Refugee Protection Regulations. This initiative aims to streamline the process for asylum seekers, providing them with the opportunity to work while their claims are being processed. Currently, the regulations impose specific conditions on when an asylum seeker is eligible for a work permit. The move reflects the government’s recognition of the need to support individuals seeking refuge and to improve their integration into Canadian society. By expediting work permits, the government hopes to alleviate some of the economic burdens faced by asylum seekers, enabling them to contribute to the economy more rapidly. The proposed changes were made public in the Canada Gazette, indicating the government's commitment to enhancing the immigration system. Stakeholders and advocacy groups are expected to provide feedback on these changes, as the government seeks to balance security concerns with humanitarian considerations. Overall, this shift represents a significant step towards a more compassionate approach to asylum seekers in Canada, aligning with the country’s values of inclusivity and community support.

Ottawa has moved to make permanent the ability for asylum claimants to obtain faster access to work permits. The move comes in the form of draft changes to the Immigration and Refugee Protection Regulations, published in the Canada Gazette the afternoon of June 19, 2026. Under the current Regulations, for an asylum seeker to qualify […]

The post Canada moves to enshrine early access to work permits for asylum seekers first appeared on CIC News.

item.title: New Brunswick invites over 660 provincial immigration candidates across six draws
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: New Brunswick, candidates, immigration, Provincial Nominee Program, Atlantic Immigration Program

Summary: New Brunswick (NB) conducted selection rounds on June 3–June 4, where a total of 669 candidates were invited through its provincial immigration programs. The invitations were issued under three specific streams of the New Brunswick Provincial Nominee Program (NBPNP) and the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP). These programs are designed to attract skilled workers and support immigration to the province. Candidates may assess their eligibility for these enhanced Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) streams to determine if they qualify for the opportunities offered. The selection round reflects New Brunswick's ongoing efforts to address labor shortages and bolster its economy by inviting individuals who can contribute to its workforce. This recent influx of invitations underscores the province's commitment to welcoming immigrants as part of its broader immigration strategy. Those interested in living and working in New Brunswick are encouraged to review the eligibility criteria for the various streams available under the NBPNP and AIP to better understand their potential immigration pathways.

New Brunswick (NB) invited 669 candidates through its provincial immigration programs in selection rounds that took place between June 3–June 4. The invitations were issued under three streams of the New Brunswick Provincial Nominee Program (NBPNP) and the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP). Assess your eligibility for enhanced PNP streams This selection round saw candidates invited […]

The post New Brunswick invites over 660 provincial immigration candidates across six draws first appeared on CIC News.

item.title: Canada just changed what counts as proof of citizenship by descent — here’s the breakdown
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, citizenship, descent, documentation, lineage

Summary: Canada's citizenship department has introduced new guidance regarding the proof of Canadian lineage required from individuals applying for citizenship by descent. This change follows instances where multiple new citizens were asked to return their citizenship certificates due to insufficient documentation. The updated guidelines have raised the bar for the supporting documentation necessary in citizenship-by-descent applications, which now require a more rigorous documentary standard. This initiative aims to ensure the integrity of the citizenship process and to prevent fraudulent claims. As a result of these changes, applicants may face stricter scrutiny of their lineage proof, leading to possible adjustments in how they prepare their documentation. The citizenship department is emphasizing thoroughness and accuracy in applications to uphold the process's credibility. These modifications reflect the government’s commitment to ensuring that citizenship rights are granted only to those with verified Canadian ties, thereby reinforcing the nation's citizenship policies. The implications of this new guidance may impact many applicants and necessitate a reevaluation of how they present their cases. Overall, the update signifies a shift toward more stringent verification standards in Canada’s citizenship by descent framework.

Canada’s citizenship department has issued new guidance on what it expects as proof of Canadian lineage from people applying for citizenship by descent, after asking multiple new Canadian citizens to return their citizenship certificates based on their submitted documentation. These changes raise the accepted supporting documentation and the documentary standard for citizenship-by-descent applications. Among other […]

The post Canada just changed what counts as proof of citizenship by descent — here’s the breakdown first appeared on CIC News.

item.title: Canada pauses processing of some citizenship-by-descent applications, clarifies rules for those under review
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, immigration, citizenship, applications, citizenship-by-descent

Summary: Canada's immigration department has halted the finalization of certain citizenship-by-descent applications. This decision comes after several individuals, who had already received approval for their citizenship, received letters instructing them to surrender their citizenship certificates. The government is currently re-examining these applications, suggesting potential issues with the approval process or applicants' eligibility. The pause affects a limited number of cases, but it raises concerns about the overall integrity of the citizenship-by-descent process. People affected by this situation may face uncertainty regarding their citizenship status and future applications. The immigration department has not yet released a timeline for how long the review will take or the reasons behind the actions taken. Individuals considering applying for citizenship-by-descent may want to seek legal advice or consultation to understand the implications of this change and how it may affect their applications. As more information becomes available, it will be crucial for applicants to stay informed about the status of the citizenship process in Canada. The situation highlights the complexities and evolving nature of immigration policies in the country.

Canada’s immigration department has temporarily stopped finalizing some new citizenship-by-descent applications, according to reporting by Global News and The Canadian Press. The pause follows letters sent to a few dozen people who had already been approved, telling them to surrender their citizenship certificates while their files are re-examined. Get a Free Consultation on Applying for […]

The post Canada pauses processing of some citizenship-by-descent applications, clarifies rules for those under review first appeared on CIC News.

item.title: Alberta launches new online tool to help foreign nationals assess AAIP eligibility
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Alberta, digital tool, immigration, questionnaire, permanent residence

Summary: Alberta has introduced a new digital tool aimed at assisting foreign nationals in identifying which streams of the Alberta Advantage Immigration Program (AAIP) they may be eligible for. This innovative tool functions by prompting users to fill out a brief questionnaire regarding their personal and professional circumstances. After submitting their answers, users receive a list of potential permanent residence pathways that fit their profile within minutes. The tool is designed to streamline the immigration process by making information more accessible and tailored to individual needs. By guiding applicants through their options, the tool supports Alberta's goal of attracting skilled workers to meet labor demands in the province. Overall, the initiative reflects the province's commitment to enhancing its immigration system to accommodate and welcome newcomers more effectively. The user-friendly nature of this digital resource is expected to not only simplify the application process but also empower foreign nationals to explore suitable immigration opportunities, thereby contributing to Alberta’s economic growth and diversity.

Alberta has launched a new digital tool to help foreign nationals determine which Alberta Advantage Immigration Program (AAIP) streams they may qualify for. Users are prompted to complete a brief questionnaire about their circumstances, and within minutes the tool lists permanent residence pathways that could be a fit based on the answers provided. Assess your […]

The post Alberta launches new online tool to help foreign nationals assess AAIP eligibility first appeared on CIC News.

item.title: The Bill C-3 paradox: Millions now qualify for Canadian citizenship, but few will apply
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: United States, Canada, citizenship, second passport, Americans

Summary: The article discusses a little-known aspect of Canada's citizenship law, which allows many Americans living in the United States to claim Canadian citizenship without realizing it. This new law suggests that these individuals may already be citizens based on specific criteria, such as having a Canadian parent or grandparent. The process requires them to gather certain documents and follow a series of steps to prove their Canadian heritage. By doing so, they could potentially obtain a second citizenship, gain access to a Canadian passport, and enjoy the rights and privileges that come with being a citizen of Canada. This revelation is significant as it implies that millions of people may unknowingly have dual citizenship, which may provide them with more opportunities and benefits in areas such as healthcare, education, and travel. The article aims to raise awareness among Americans about their eligibility and encourages them to explore their familial connections to Canada and take action if they choose to obtain their second citizenship. Overall, it highlights an important and often overlooked opportunity for many in the U.S. to connect with their Canadian roots.

The United States is now home to millions of secret Canadians who have no idea that they are currently eligible for a second citizenship, passport, and all the rights that come with it. Under Canada’s new citizenship law, these Americans are already citizens; they just need to follow the trail of clues to prove it […]

The post The Bill C-3 paradox: Millions now qualify for Canadian citizenship, but few will apply first appeared on CIC News.

item.title: Canada moved the goalposts for proof of citizenship applicants, lawyers say
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canadian citizenship, descent, certificates, surrender, regulations

Summary: On June 13, 2026, the Registrar of Citizenship sent letters to an undisclosed number of Canadian citizens by descent, instructing them to surrender their citizenship certificates. This directive was issued under the authority of Citizenship Regulations 26(1), indicating potential infractions concerning the rules of citizenship that the recipients claim were never disclosed to them. The affected individuals may find themselves in a distressing situation, as they are being asked to relinquish proof of their citizenship status without prior knowledge of any violations on their part. The implications of this enforcement raise questions about transparency in communication from the Canadian government and the processes involved in determining adherence to citizenship regulations. The citizenship by descent process is designed to ensure that individuals born outside Canada but with Canadian parentage can claim their citizenship. However, the recent actions suggest systemic issues that could affect many individuals, undermining their rights and sense of security regarding their citizenship status. As this situation unfolds, it will be essential to monitor how the government addresses these concerns and whether any recourse will be available for those impacted by the requested surrender of their certificates.

Canadian citizenship certificate holders have been instructed to surrender their certificates for having broken rules they were never told. On June 13, 2026, an unknown number of Canadian citizen by descent certificate holders received letters from the Registrar of Citizenship requiring them to surrender their certificates, under the authority of Citizenship Regulations 26(1), as their […]

The post Canada moved the goalposts for proof of citizenship applicants, lawyers say first appeared on CIC News.

item.title: These temporary residence applicants are seeing shorter wait times
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canada, IRCC, work permit, processing times, applicants

Summary: On June 17, Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) updated its processing time estimates for various temporary residence categories. The update revealed shorter wait times for some applicants, reflecting improvements in processing efficiency. Specifically, in-Canada work permit timelines show significant progress, with a decrease of over two weeks in wait times for applicants. Additionally, submissions for work permits from India and Nigeria have seen a reduction in processing times as well, with one-week decreases reported for those applications. This positive trend in processing times suggests a growing capacity within the IRCC to handle applications more effectively. The reduction in wait times is likely to benefit many prospective applicants seeking temporary residence in Canada, making the transition smoother and more efficient. Overall, the updated processing times signal the IRCC’s commitment to enhancing service delivery and responding to the needs of applicants engaged in Temporary Residence programs.

On June 17, Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) updated its processing time estimates for various temporary residence categories, showing shorter wait times for some applicants. In-Canada work permit timelines continue to improve, with wait times falling by more than two weeks. India- and Nigeria-based work permit submissions also experienced one-week reductions. Schedule a Free […]

The post These temporary residence applicants are seeing shorter wait times first appeared on CIC News.

item.title: Forced surrender of Canadian citizenship certificates may be unconstitutional, experts say
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Canadian citizenship, constitutional rights, Registrar, surrender, investigation

Summary: On June 13, 2026, the Canadian government, through the Registrar of Citizenship, issued mass letters to individuals born outside Canada. These letters demanded the immediate surrender of their Canadian citizenship certificates. This action was taken while the cases of these individuals were being investigated, raising serious concerns about potential violations of constitutional rights. Many holders of proof of Canadian citizenship certificates felt alarmed and confused by this unexpected requirement. The government's abrupt move has led to widespread outrage and calls for clarification regarding the legal justification behind the mass investigation and potential repercussions for those affected. Critics argue that the measure undermines the trust citizens have in governmental processes, particularly in matters concerning their citizenship status. Legal experts are closely watching the situation, questioning the constitutionality of such a sweeping demand without due process. Individuals impacted by this order have expressed fears of loss of identity and legal rights, urging for immediate legal recourse and assurances from government officials. Overall, this unprecedented action by the federal government has ignited a debate about citizenship rights and the need for safeguarding individual freedoms in Canada.

Holders of proof of Canadian citizenship certificates may have had their constitutional rights violated by the federal government. On June 13, 2026, the Registrar of citizenship issued letters en masse to persons who had been born outside Canada, requiring the immediate surrender of their Canadian citizenship certificates while their cases were being investigated, as authorized […]

The post Forced surrender of Canadian citizenship certificates may be unconstitutional, experts say first appeared on CIC News.

item.title: IRCC’s total application backlog drops to lowest level since July 2025
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: immigration, backlog, IRCC, Express Entry, decrease

Summary: The immigration department's total application backlog has decreased significantly, with April 2026 figures indicating it is at its lowest since July 2025. Noteworthy changes highlighted in the Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) update include a record low backlog of 9% specifically for the Express Entry program. Additionally, there has been a 5% decrease in the overall backlog, reflecting the government's ongoing efforts to streamline immigration processing and reduce waiting times for applicants. This continuous decline in backlog levels suggests progress in addressing previous delays and improving the efficiency of the immigration system. As a result, applicants may experience a more timely resolution of their immigration status. Overall, these updates signify a positive trend for IRCC as it works to enhance its operations and support prospective immigrants while responding to the growing demand for immigration services. The current state of the backlog underscores a commitment to improving processes and welcoming new residents more effectively.

The immigration department’s total application backlog has continued to shrink, with newly released figures for April 2026 showing it reached its lowest level since July 2025. The most notable changes seen in Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada’s (IRCC’s) backlog update include: A record low of 9% for the Express Entry backlog; A 5% decrease in […]

The post IRCC’s total application backlog drops to lowest level since July 2025 first appeared on CIC News.

item.title: Canada proposes new regulations to modernize the asylum process and support timely decisions
item.content:encoded: Building on recent measures to strengthen Canada’s immigration and asylum systems, and recent decreases in asylum claim volumes, the Government of Canada is taking further action to maintain a fair, orderly and well-managed asylum system.

item.title: Canada strengthens borders while supporting economic and tourism ties with Saint-Pierre-et-Miquelon
item.content:encoded: Canada and the French overseas collectivity of Saint-Pierre-et-Miquelon share strong travel, tourism and economic ties, particularly in Atlantic Canada. We work closely to support safe, efficient travel while strengthening the security and integrity of our shared travel routes.

item.title: Canada strengthens Indo-Pacific ties through changes to visa requirements for eligible travellers from Indonesia and Malaysia
item.content:encoded: As Canada continues to deepen its trade, investment and strategic partnerships across the Indo Pacific, the Honourable Lena Metlege Diab, Minister of Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship, announced today that Canada will make changes to visa requirements for eligible citizens from Indonesia and Malaysia starting tomorrow, May 26, 2026, at 5:30 a.m. Eastern Time.

item.title: Building the workforce needed to deliver more homes in Halifax
item.content:encoded: In Budget 2025, we outlined our plan to build Canada Strong.

item.title: Canada and global partners take action to advance migration priorities
item.content:encoded: Canada is working with partners around the world to strengthen migration systems and address shared challenges. This week, the Honourable Lena Metlege Diab, Minister of Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship, led Canada’s delegation to the International Migration Review Forum (IMRF) 2026 at the United Nations Headquarters in New York.

item.title: Canada strengthens regulation of immigration and citizenship consultants
item.content:encoded: The Government of Canada is strengthening protections for people seeking immigration and citizenship advice by improving access to trustworthy, quality representation. Today, the Honourable Lena Metlege Diab, Minister of Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship, announced new regulations that have come into force to enhance the oversight of immigration and citizenship consultants in Canada.

item.title: Filling labour gaps in smaller communities by accelerating permanent residence for 33,000 workers
item.content:encoded: Canada continues to bring our immigration system to sustainable levels and focus immigration on the areas where it has the greatest impact. This means supporting rural and remote communities that are experiencing labour shortages in key economic sectors.

item.title: Minister Diab highlights sport investments from Spring Economic Update 2026: Canada Strong for All to build stronger and safer communities
item.content:encoded: In Budget 2025, the Government of Canada outlined its plan to build Canada strong. Since then, we have moved fast to build the major infrastructure, homes and industries that grow Canada’s economy and create lasting prosperity; empower Canadians with better careers and a more affordable life; and protect our communities, our borders and our way of life.

item.title: Minister Diab concludes Citizenship Week 2026 by welcoming new Canadians
item.content:encoded: Citizenship Week is an opportunity to reflect on what it means to be Canadian, celebrate the people who choose to call Canada home and recognize the Indigenous Peoples who have lived on this land since time immemorial.

item.title: Canada launches measures to support those impacted by domestic natural disasters
item.content:encoded: Canada continues to face the growing impacts of climate change, with extreme weather conditions becoming more frequent and severe. Communities across the country have responded to these challenges with resilience and strength, and the Government of Canada remains committed to supporting those affected.

item.title: Canada extends certain work permit measures for Ukrainians
item.content:encoded: As Russia's illegal war against Ukraine continues, Canada continues to support Ukrainian people who are here contributing to our communities and labour market.

item.title: Canada begins new “30 days or free” guarantee for passport processing
item.content:encoded: The Government of Canada is strengthening accountability for passport processing by introducing a new guarantee for passport services.

item.title: Canada is celebrating the Francophonie and investing in new initiatives to promote Francophone immigration outside Quebec
item.content:encoded: The Government of Canada has made supporting Francophone and Acadian minority communities outside Quebec a top priority. The goal is to attract and integrate skilled French-speaking and bilingual immigrants from around the world to boost population growth, meet labour needs and support economic development in these communities.

item.title: New immigration measure to support Quebec workers and employers
item.content:encoded: The Government of Canada is restoring control of our immigration system while attracting and retaining those with the skills and experience needed to drive our economic growth.

item.title: Canada prioritizes top talent in 2026 immigration Express Entry categories
item.content:encoded: Canada is taking back control to return immigration to sustainable levels, while ensuring it continues to support the economy. As global competition for skilled workers intensifies, Canada’s immigration system is focused on attracting and retaining highly skilled immigrants through the International Talent Attraction Strategy.

item.title: Canada exceeded its Francophone immigration target for 2025
item.content:encoded: Francophone and Acadian minority communities outside Quebec contribute to the economy, culture and vitality of the French language in Canada. Francophone immigration remains essential and aims to address labour needs and to support communities across the country.

item.title: New citizenship rules for Canadians born or adopted abroad are now in effect
item.content:encoded: The Government of Canada is committed to making Canadian citizenship law fair, clear and reflective of how Canadian families live today, both in and outside Canada.

item.title: Targeted immigration measures to boost Canada’s supply of doctors
item.content:encoded: In support of the Budget 2025 commitment to fill critical labour gaps with high-skilled talent, the Honourable Lena Metlege Diab, Minister of Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship, and Maggie Chi, Parliamentary Secretary to the Minister of Health, today announced new measures to support Canada’s health care system by making the path to permanent residence simpler for international doctors.

item.title: Canada to welcome thousands of visitors for the FIFA World Cup 26™ - Don’t wait, apply early
item.content:encoded: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) is proud to join Canadians in celebrating Canada’s role as a co host of the FIFA World Cup 26™ with the United States and Mexico.

item.title: Canada invests in Francophone communities and helps build their future by attracting top global talent
item.content:encoded: As announced in Budget 2025, the Government of Canada wants to focus immigration where it delivers the greatest impact to fill labour gaps, strengthen key economic sectors and support communities across the country. This includes prioritizing Francophone immigration outside Quebec, helping to enhance the vitality of Francophone and Acadian minority communities, and making the country more attractive to the world’s top Francophone and bilingual talents.

item.title: Canada lifts the visa requirement for Qatari citizens
item.content:encoded: The Honourable Lena Metlege Diab, Minister of Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship today announced that Canada has lifted the visa requirement for citizens of Qatar. This change took effect this morning at 5:30 a.m. EST.

item.title: Canada reaches important milestone in restoring fairness and clarity to citizenship by descent
item.content:encoded: November 21, 2025—Ottawa—Canada has taken an important step toward modernizing its citizenship laws. Canadians excluded under outdated rules will have a fair, clear way to pass citizenship on to their children born or adopted outside Canada.

item.title: Canada’s new Climate Competitiveness Strategy
item.content:encoded: Climate action is not only a moral responsibility—it’s an economic necessity.

item.title: Canada extends temporary measures for Palestinians in Canada
item.content:encoded: Canada extends temporary measures for Palestinians in Canada

item.title: Canada invests in services to support economic integration and fill labour market shortages
item.content:encoded: Canada’s new government has a mandate to build a strong economy by attracting the best talent in the world and filling key labour shortages.
